sn41.6:0.3: Sutta With Kāmabhū (On the Cessation of Perception & Feeling)
sn41.6:1.1: On one occasion Ven. Kāmabhū was staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove.
sn41.6:1.2: Then Citta the householder went to him and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to Ven. Kāmabhū,
sn41.6:1.3: “Venerable sir, how many (types of) fabrications are there?”
sn41.6:1.6: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:1.7: “But what are bodily-fabrications? What are verbal fabrications? What are mental fabrications?”
sn41.6:1.8: “In-&-out breaths are bodily fabrications. Directed thought & evaluation are verbal fabrications. Perceptions & feelings are mental fabrications.”
sn41.6:2.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:2.2: “But why are in-&-out breaths bodily fabrications? Why are directed thought & evaluation verbal fabrications? Why are perceptions & feelings mental fabrications?”
sn41.6:2.3: “In-&-out breaths are bodily; these are things tied up with the body. That’s why in-&-out breaths are bodily fabrications.
sn41.6:2.4: Having first directed one’s thoughts and made an evaluation, one then breaks out into speech. That’s why directed thought & evaluation are verbal fabrications.
sn41.6:2.5: Perceptions & feelings are mental; these are things tied up with the mind. That’s why perceptions & feelings are mental fabrications.”
sn41.6:3.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:3.2: “Now, how does the attainment of the cessation of perception & feeling come about?”
sn41.6:3.3: “The thought does not occur to a monk as he is attaining the cessation of perception & feeling that
sn41.6:3.4: ‘I am about to attain the cessation of perception & feeling’ or that ‘I am attaining the cessation of perception & feeling’ or that ‘I have attained the cessation of perception & feeling.’
sn41.6:3.5: Instead, the way his mind has previously been developed leads him to that state.”
sn41.6:4.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:4.2: “When a monk is attaining the cessation of perception & feeling, which things cease first: bodily fabrications, verbal fabrications, or mental fabrications?”
sn41.6:4.3: “When a monk is attaining the cessation of perception & feeling, verbal fabrications cease first, then bodily fabrications, then mental fabrications.”
sn41.6:5.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:5.2: “What is the difference between a monk who has died & passed away and a monk who has attained the cessation of perception & feeling?”
sn41.6:5.3: “In the case of a monk who has died & passed away, his bodily fabrication has ceased & subsided, verbal fabrication has ceased & subsided, mental fabrication has ceased & subsided, his life force is totally ended, his heat is dissipated, and his faculties are shut down.
sn41.6:5.4: But in the case of a monk who has attained the cessation of perception & feeling, his bodily fabrication has ceased & subsided, verbal fabrication has ceased & subsided, mental fabrication has ceased & subsided, his life force is not ended, his heat is not dissipated, and his faculties are bright & clear.
sn41.6:5.5: This is the difference between a monk who has died & passed away and a monk who has attained the cessation of perception & feeling.”
sn41.6:6.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:6.2: “Now, how does emergence from the cessation of perception & feeling come about?”
sn41.6:6.3: “The thought does not occur to a monk as he is emerging from the cessation of perception & feeling that
sn41.6:6.4: ‘I am about to emerge from the cessation of perception & feeling’ or that ‘I am emerging from the cessation of perception & feeling’ or that ‘I have emerged from the cessation of perception & feeling.’
sn41.6:6.5: Instead, the way his mind has previously been developed leads him to that state.”
sn41.6:7.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:7.2: “When a monk is emerging from the cessation of perception & feeling, which things arise first: bodily fabrications, verbal fabrications, or mental fabrications?”
sn41.6:7.3: “When a monk is emerging from the cessation of perception & feeling, mental fabrications arise first, then bodily fabrications, then verbal fabrications.”
sn41.6:8.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:8.2: “When a monk has emerged from the cessation of perception & feeling, how many contacts make contact?”
sn41.6:8.3: “When a monk has emerged from the cessation of perception & feeling, three contacts make contact:
sn41.6:8.4: contact with emptiness, contact with the themeless, & contact with the undirected.”
sn41.6:9.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:9.2: “When a monk has emerged from the cessation of perception & feeling, to what does his mind lean, to what does it tend, to what does it incline?”
sn41.6:9.3: “When a monk has emerged from the cessation of perception & feeling, his mind leans to seclusion, tends to seclusion, inclines to seclusion.”
sn41.6:10.1: “Very good, venerable sir.” And, delighting in and approving of Ven. Kāmabhū’s answer, Citta asked him a further question:
sn41.6:10.2: “How many mental qualities are of great help in the attainment of the cessation of perception & feeling?”
sn41.6:10.3: “Actually, householder, you have asked last what should have been asked first.
sn41.6:10.5: Two qualities are of great help in the attainment of the cessation of perception & feeling:
